1.description: Sales of single-family homes. The graphs show the relation between the number of years between sales of single-family homes and (i) the log return (not annualized) from owning that home over the period (left panel) and (ii) the frequency of home sales (right panel). The solid smooth line in the left panel depicts a spline estimator, with the 90% confidence interval given by dashed lines. To remove the effect of home price appreciation on housing returns over this period, we adjust holding-period returns using year-quarter fixed effects for the time of purchase and the time of sale. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Changes in lead venture capital investors. The graphs show the relation between the number of years between changes in lead VC investors of a startup company and (i) the log return (not annualized) from investing in the startup over the period (left panel) and (ii) the frequency of changes in lead investors (right panel). The solid smooth line in the left panel depicts a spline estimator, with the 90% confidence interval given by dashed lines. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Sales of construction equipment. The graphs show the relation between the number of years between sales of construction equipment and (i) the log return (not annualized) from owning that equipment over the period (left panel) and (ii) the frequency of equipment sales (right panel). The solid smooth line in the left panel depicts a spline estimator, with the 90% confidence interval given by dashed lines. To remove the effect of physical depreciation on equipment returns, we adjust holding-period returns using fixed effects for the equipment age at the time of purchase and the time of sale. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Owner's value and strategy in an equilibrium in whichœÑH=‚àû$\tau _H=\infty$. In response to sustained negative information, the owner's value of the asset,VL$V_{L}$, declines initially as it becomes less and less likely that the asset is high quality. If the payoff breakthrough has not arrived bytÃÇL$\hat{t}_L$, the owner's value falls far enough that she begins selling strategically, leading to a gradual decline in the market price,p$p$. Eventually, the probability of a strategic sale shrinks, and the market price and the value of ownership recover. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Market dynamics with low liquidity. When the arrival rate of the liquidity shockœÄ$\pi$is small, the likelihood of a strategic sale relative to a liquidity sale is large, and as a result, the hump-shape in the distribution of strategic selling leads to a hump-shape in the total likelihood of trade. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Market dynamics with high liquidity andœÑH=‚àû$\tau _H=\infty$. When the probability of a liquidity sale is high, the market pricep$p$exhibits a U-shape, while the total likelihood of trade is declining over the owner's length of ownership. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
